title: PEFT: Training a Fraction
concept: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) keeps the pretrained backbone frozen, updating only a tiny fraction (e.g. < 1%) of additional parameters for the new task.

exercises.prompt: What is the main downside of traditional, full fine-tuning on massive AI models?

options.text: It is extremely fast and uses almost no computer memory.
It requires updating all parameters, which is very slow and storage-heavy.
It only updates the final output layer of the model.

exercises.explain: Standard fine-tuning updates every single weight in a model, which requires massive amounts of computing power and storage.

cards.title: Why Rebuild the Whole Model?
cards.body: Updating a massive modern AI model is like trying to rebuild a skyscraper just to paint one bedroom a different color. It's incredibly slow and expensive. What if we could just add a tiny, adjustable extension instead?

cards.title: What is PEFT?
cards.body: Instead of training all billions of connections in a model, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) freezes the original model and only trains a tiny fraction (typically less than 1%) of extra parameters. This saves massive amounts of computer memory and storage!

cards.title: The Library Notepad
cards.body: Imagine a giant library where all the millions of books are locked behind glass cases. You aren't allowed to rewrite any of those books. Instead, you are given a tiny notepad to write down custom updates or specialized local directions. The massive library (pretrained model) stays completely untouched, and you only spend energy updating your small notepad (PEFT parameters).

cards.title: Real-World Saving
cards.body: Instead of saving a brand new 100-gigabyte model for every task (like medical writing, legal advice, or translating code), PEFT lets you share the same frozen base model and save tiny 50-megabyte adapter files for each specific job.

cards.title: Does Less Training Mean Worse Results?
cards.body: People often think that if you don't train the entire model, the performance will be terrible. In reality, PEFT techniques like LoRA can match the performance of full training while updating less than 1% of the weights!

cards.title: Full Training vs. PEFT
cards.body: Compare how traditional full fine-tuning compares to PEFT when adapting to a new task.

left.text: Updates 100% of parameters. Requires massive hardware, memory, and creates huge files.

right.text: Freezes base parameters. Updates < 1% of parameters, saving massive storage and memory.

exercises.prompt: What fraction of parameters are trained?

options.text: A tiny fraction, often less than 1%

exercises.explain: PEFT specifically targets training a tiny fraction (often under 1%) of the parameters to save computational power and storage space.

exercises.prompt: True or False: PEFT makes fine-tuning much cheaper because it requires far less computer memory than updating the entire model.
exercises.explain: True! PEFT avoids modifying the billions of original parameters, allowing you to fine-tune using much less GPU memory and storage.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how PEFT handles the base model's original parameters.
exercises.sentence: PEFT keeps the original pre-trained model weights completely [blank] while training only a tiny set of new parameters.
exercises.explain: In PEFT, the vast majority of the original model parameters are frozen (kept unchanged) while only a tiny set of new parameters is modified.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios is using Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)?

options.text: An AI that retrains all 175 billion connections from scratch to learn legal terminology.
An AI that locks its main 175-billion-connection brain and only trains a tiny 1-million-parameter adapter layer for legal terms.
An AI that randomly deletes half of its brain to save disk space.

exercises.explain: The second choice represents PEFT because it freeze-locks the massive base model and only updates a tiny extra adapter layer. The first is traditional full fine-tuning.

exercises.prompt: You want to fine-tune a massive AI model but only have a single, modest computer. Why is Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) the perfect solution here?

options.text: It keeps the massive base model frozen and only trains a tiny fraction of new parameters.
It deletes 99% of the model to make it run faster on cheap hardware.
It retrains every single parameter from scratch using a smaller learning rate.
It converts text data into images before training begins.

exercises.explain: By freezing the vast majority of the original parameters and only training a tiny fraction (often less than 1%), PEFT allows you to adapt large models quickly using minimal computer memory and storage!
